topics.text: ★ EXAM: Big-O O(1) constant — array[i] access, hash lookup. Input size irrelevant
★ EXAM: Big-O O(log n) — binary search halves problem each step. 1M items = 20 steps
★ EXAM: Big-O O(n) — linear scan, must check every element once
★ EXAM: Big-O O(n log n) — merge sort, quick sort avg. Best possible comparison sort
★ EXAM: Big-O O(n²) — nested loops. Bubble/selection/insertion sort worst case
★ EXAM: Big-O rules — drop constants O(3n)=O(n), drop lower terms O(n²+n)=O(n²)
⚠️ TRAP: Big-O is WORST case, not average. Big-Theta Θ is tight bound both ways
Space complexity: extra memory used. Merge sort O(n), quick sort O(log n) stack
vs: Array vs Linked List — Array: contiguous, O(1) access; List: O(n) access, O(1) insert at head
★ EXAM: Stack LIFO — push/pop O(1). Used for function calls, undo, expression eval
★ EXAM: Queue FIFO — enqueue/dequeue O(1). Circular queue: (rear+1)%size wraps around
⚠️ TRAP: Simple array queue dequeue is O(n) — must shift. Use circular array or linked list
★ EXAM: BST — left < root < right. Search O(log n) avg. O(n) worst if skewed (like linked list)
vs: BST vs AVL — AVL self-balances with rotations. Height diff ≤ 1. Search always O(log n)
★ EXAM: Heap — max-heap: parent ≥ children. Insert/extract O(log n). Build heap O(n)
★ EXAM: Hash table — O(1) avg lookup. Collision via chaining (linked list) or open addressing
vs: Chaining vs Open Addressing — chaining uses external lists; open probes within table
★ EXAM: In-order BST traversal = sorted output. Pre-order = root first. Post-order = root last
vs: Adjacency Matrix O(V²) space O(1) edge check vs List O(V+E) space O(V) edge check
vs: BFS (queue, shortest path, O(V+E)) vs DFS (stack/recursion, less memory, O(V+E))
★ EXAM: Bubble Sort — compare adjacent, swap. Best O(n) sorted, Avg/Worst O(n²). Stable
★ EXAM: Selection Sort — find min, place it. Best/Avg/Worst all O(n²). NOT stable
★ EXAM: Insertion Sort — insert into sorted portion. Best O(n), Avg/Worst O(n²). Stable
★ EXAM: Merge Sort — divide & conquer. ALL cases O(n log n). Space O(n). Stable
★ EXAM: Quick Sort — partition around pivot. Best/Avg O(n log n), Worst O(n²). NOT stable
⚠️ TRAP: Quick Sort worst O(n²) when pivot is always min/max (sorted input + first pivot)
⚠️ TRAP: Merge Sort is stable. Quick Sort is NOT. Stability = equal elements keep order
★ EXAM: Binary Search — sorted array, halves each step. O(log n). Returns mid when found
Heap Sort — build max-heap then extract-max n times. O(n log n) always. Not stable. In-place
Counting Sort — non-comparison. O(n+k) where k=range. Stable. Only for integers
⚠️ TRAP: Comparison sorts cannot beat O(n log n). Counting/Radix sorts can be O(n) but limited
Linked list operations: insert head O(1), insert tail O(n) unless tail pointer, delete O(n) search
vs: Singly vs Doubly linked list — doubly has prev pointer, O(1) delete if node known
Circular linked list — last node points to first. Used in round-robin scheduling
★ EXAM: Priority Queue — each element has priority. Implemented via heap. O(log n) insert/remove
★ EXAM: Data types — int (4B), float (4B, ~7 digits), double (8B, ~15 digits), char (1B), bool
⚠️ TRAP: Float 0.1+0.2 ≠ 0.3 due to binary representation. Use epsilon for comparison
★ EXAM: Switch-case must use int/char/enum. Requires "break" or falls through to next case
vs: While vs Do-While — while checks first (0 runs possible); do-while runs body then checks
★ EXAM: File I/O modes — r(read), w(overwrite), a(append), rb(read binary), wb(write binary)
⚠️ TRAP: Mode "w" instantly deletes existing file content. Use "a" to preserve and append
vs: Recursion vs Iteration — recursion uses O(n) stack space; iteration uses O(1) space
⚠️ TRAP: Missing base case in recursion → infinite calls → stack overflow error
vs: Array (fixed size, contiguous) vs Vector (dynamic, doubles when full, O(n) resize copy)
★ EXAM: Scope — local vars in stack frame (die with function); global/static persist in data segment
★ EXAM: Pass-by-value copies data; pass-by-reference passes address — changes affect original
★ EXAM: Debugging — breakpoints pause execution; stepping runs one line at a time
★ EXAM: High cohesion (module does one thing well) + low coupling (modules independent)
★ EXAM: DRY principle — Don't Repeat Yourself. Extract common code into reusable functions
★ EXAM: Dry run — trace code line-by-line with variable trace table to find logic errors
Common algorithms — GCD (Euclidean), factorial n!, Fibonacci sequence, palindrome check
★ EXAM: Encapsulation — private fields + public getters/setters. Prevents invalid state
★ EXAM: Inheritance — child "extends" parent, reuses code. Java=single, C++=multiple
★ EXAM: Polymorphism — overloading (compile-time, diff params) vs overriding (runtime, child replaces)
⚠️ TRAP: Overriding is RUNTIME dispatch. Overloading is COMPILE-TIME resolution. Don't confuse
★ EXAM: Abstraction — hide implementation, show interface. Abstract class cannot be instantiated
vs: Abstract class (partial code, single inherit) vs Interface (pure contract, multiple inherit)
⚠️ TRAP: Interfaces have NO instance variables, NO constructors — only constants + abstract methods
★ EXAM: Constructor — called on "new". No return type. Can be overloaded for diff initializations
★ EXAM: Destructor — C++ ~ClassName(). Java has no destructor (GC + close()/try-with-resources)
★ EXAM: Exception — try (risky code) → catch (handle error) → finally (ALWAYS runs, cleanup)
⚠️ TRAP: "finally" block executes even if try/catch contains "return" statement
★ EXAM: GUI events — Button fires ActionListener → handler method executes on event thread
★ EXAM: UML class diagram — top: ClassName, mid: -attributes, bottom: +methods(). +public -private
vs: Association (weak "uses") vs Composition (strong "owns" — part destroyed with whole)
★ EXAM: Static members — belong to class not instance. Shared memory. Accessed via ClassName.member
Concrete class — implements ALL abstract methods. Can be instantiated with "new"
vs: Checked exceptions (must handle) vs Unchecked (RuntimeException, optional handling)
★ EXAM: HTTP cycle — Client request → DNS resolve → TCP handshake → Server response
★ EXAM: HTTP GET=read, POST=create, PUT=replace, PATCH=partial update, DELETE=remove
vs: GET (params in URL, cacheable, limited size) vs POST (body, not cached, unlimited)
★ EXAM: Status 200=OK, 201=Created, 301=Redirect, 400=Bad Request, 401=Unauthorized, 404=Not Found, 500=Server Error
★ EXAM: DOM — tree of HTML nodes. document.getElementById() returns element for JS manipulation
HTML semantic tags: header/nav/main/section/article/footer — describe meaning not appearance
★ EXAM: CSS box model — content → padding → border → margin. box-sizing: border-box includes all
CSS specificity: inline(1000) > id(100) > class(10) > tag(1). !important overrides all
vs: AJAX (XHR, callbacks) vs Fetch API (modern, Promises, cleaner syntax)
⚠️ TRAP: AJAX is a TECHNIQUE, not a language. Uses JavaScript + XMLHttpRequest
vs: Cookie (client, sent every request, 4KB) vs Session (server, needs session ID) vs localStorage (client, 5MB, never sent)
⚠️ TRAP: localStorage is NEVER sent to server automatically, unlike cookies
★ EXAM: Same-Origin Policy — browser blocks cross-origin requests. CORS headers allow exceptions
vs: REST (stateless, JSON, HTTP methods) vs SOAP (XML, WSDL contract, heavier)
★ EXAM: SSL/TLS — asymmetric key exchange → symmetric session encryption. HTTPS = HTTP + TLS
⚠️ TRAP: HTTPS encrypts transit only. Does NOT prevent XSS, CSRF, or SQL injection
★ EXAM: MVC — Model(data/logic), View(UI), Controller(input handler). Separation of concerns
★ EXAM: Primary Key — unique, NOT NULL. Foreign Key — references PK in another table
★ EXAM: INNER JOIN — only matching rows in both tables returned
vs: LEFT JOIN (all left + matches) vs RIGHT JOIN (all right + matches) vs FULL (all rows)
⚠️ TRAP: LEFT JOIN returns NULLs for non-matching right side — this is normal, not an error
★ EXAM: SQL SELECT syntax: SELECT col FROM table WHERE cond GROUP BY col HAVING cond ORDER BY col
vs: WHERE filters ROWS before grouping. HAVING filters GROUPS after GROUP BY
★ EXAM: Aggregates — COUNT(), SUM(), AVG(), MAX(), MIN(). Used with GROUP BY
vs: DDL (CREATE/ALTER/DROP — structure) vs DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/SELECT — data)
★ EXAM: ACID — Atomicity(all/nothing), Consistency(rules), Isolation(concurrency), Durability(permanent)
⚠️ TRAP: Isolation levels trade consistency for speed. Read Uncommitted → dirty reads
★ EXAM: Transactions — BEGIN → operations → COMMIT(save) or ROLLBACK(undo all)
★ EXAM: Index = B-tree for fast lookup. Speeds SELECT, slows INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
vs: Clustered index (reorders rows, 1 per table) vs Non-clustered (pointer, multiple allowed)
⚠️ TRAP: Composite index (A,B) helps queries on (A) or (A,B), NOT queries on (B) alone
vs: Stored Procedure (executes DML, no return required) vs Function (MUST return, no DML)
vs: MongoDB (NoSQL, document, flexible schema) vs MySQL (SQL, relational, fixed schema)
★ EXAM: 1NF — atomic values (no lists in cells), unique rows, primary key exists
★ EXAM: 2NF — 1NF + no partial dependency (non-key depends on FULL composite key)
★ EXAM: 3NF — 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key depends only on key, not other non-key)
★ EXAM: BCNF — every determinant is a candidate key. Stricter than 3NF
⚠️ TRAP: Denormalization intentionally adds redundancy for read speed at cost of write anomalies
★ EXAM: ER diagram — Rectangle=entity, Ellipse=attribute, Diamond=relationship, Lines=connections
★ EXAM: Cardinality — 1:1, 1:N, M:N. M:N must be decomposed via junction table
Weak entity — depends on strong entity. Has no PK of its own. Uses partial key + owner FK
⚠️ TRAP: FK CAN be NULL (optional relationship) unless explicitly constrained NOT NULL
★ EXAM: Referential integrity — FK must match existing PK or be NULL. CASCADE DELETE propagates
vs: ER diagram (conceptual design) vs Relational schema (implementation tables)
★ EXAM: Normalization purpose — eliminate redundancy, prevent insert/update/delete anomalies
Subquery — SELECT inside SELECT. Correlated subquery references outer query row-by-row
Views — virtual table from saved query. Simplifies complex joins. Does not store data
★ EXAM: SQL injection prevention — use parameterized queries / prepared statements, never concatenate
★ EXAM: Android lifecycle: onCreate→onStart→onResume→[running]→onPause→onStop→onDestroy
⚠️ TRAP: Screen rotation destroys + recreates Activity. Save state via onSaveInstanceState()
★ EXAM: 4 components — Activity(UI), Service(background), BroadcastReceiver(events), ContentProvider(data sharing)
vs: Explicit intent (names target component) vs Implicit intent (declares action, system resolves)
★ EXAM: Normal permissions (auto-granted at install) vs Dangerous permissions (runtime user approval)
★ EXAM: SQLite — embedded serverless DB. SQLiteOpenHelper manages creation/versioning
★ EXAM: APK build: .java→.class→classes.dex→APK (zipped with resources + manifest)
⚠️ TRAP: Service runs on MAIN thread by default. Use IntentService/WorkManager for long tasks
★ EXAM: BroadcastReceiver — listens for system events (battery low, boot, network change)
★ EXAM: ContentProvider — secure inter-app data sharing interface (query, insert, update, delete)
Mobile constraints — limited battery, memory, CPU, variable network. Code must be resource-efficient
★ EXAM: ProGuard — shrinks, optimizes, obfuscates bytecode. Reduces APK size, deters reverse engineering
⚠️ TRAP: Storing credentials in plaintext SharedPreferences = insecure. Use EncryptedSharedPreferences
vs: Fragment (reusable UI piece inside Activity) vs Activity (full screen window with own lifecycle)
★ EXAM: AndroidManifest.xml declares components, permissions, min SDK, app entry point
RecyclerView — efficient scrolling list. Uses ViewHolder pattern to recycle off-screen views
★ EXAM: 5 process states — New→Ready→Running→Waiting(blocked)→Terminated
⚠️ TRAP: Process cannot go Waiting→Running directly. Must return to Ready queue first
★ EXAM: PCB stores PID, state, PC, registers, memory maps. Saved/restored on context switch
vs: Process (own memory space, heavy) vs Thread (shared memory within process, light)
★ EXAM: FCFS — First Come First Served. Non-preemptive. Convoy effect (short waits for long)
★ EXAM: SJF — Shortest Job First. Optimal avg wait time. Can starve long processes
⚠️ TRAP: SJF requires knowing burst time in advance — not always possible in practice
★ EXAM: Round Robin — time quantum for each process cyclically. Fair. Prevents starvation
⚠️ TRAP: RR quantum too large → degrades to FCFS. Too small → excessive context switch overhead
★ EXAM: Priority scheduling — highest priority first. Aging = increase priority over time to prevent starvation
vs: Preemptive (can interrupt running process) vs Non-preemptive (waits until process finishes/blocks)
Multilevel Queue — separate queues for process types. Foreground (interactive) > Background (batch)
★ EXAM: Context switch — save current PCB, load next PCB. Pure overhead (no useful work)
★ EXAM: Turnaround time = completion - arrival. Waiting time = turnaround - burst
vs: Kernel mode (unrestricted hardware access) vs User mode (restricted, protected)
★ EXAM: System call — user process requests kernel service. Switches user→kernel mode
★ EXAM: Deadlock 4 conditions (ALL must hold): Mutual Exclusion, Hold&Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
★ EXAM: Deadlock prevention — eliminate one condition. Deadlock avoidance — Banker's algorithm
★ EXAM: Banker's: check Need ≤ Available. Simulate allocation. If safe sequence exists → grant
Deadlock detection — allow it, detect via resource allocation graph, recover by killing process
vs: Internal fragmentation (wasted space inside partition) vs External (scattered free gaps)
★ EXAM: Memory fit — First-Fit(first block), Best-Fit(tightest), Worst-Fit(largest leftover)
★ EXAM: Paging — fixed-size frames. Page table maps logical→physical. No external fragmentation
⚠️ TRAP: Paging eliminates EXTERNAL fragmentation but introduces INTERNAL fragmentation
vs: Paging (fixed blocks, physical) vs Segmentation (variable logical units — code/data/stack)
★ EXAM: Virtual memory — disk as RAM extension. Process can exceed physical RAM size
★ EXAM: Page fault — page not in RAM → OS loads from disk. Expensive I/O operation
★ EXAM: Page replacement — FIFO(oldest out), LRU(least recent), Optimal(farthest future use)
⚠️ TRAP: Belady's Anomaly — FIFO can get MORE faults with MORE frames. LRU is immune
★ EXAM: Thrashing — excessive paging. CPU utilization drops. Working set model prevents it
vs: FAT32 (4GB max file, no security) vs NTFS (large files, journaling, encryption, ACLs)
Semaphore — integer sync variable. wait(P) decrements, signal(V) increments. Controls concurrency
★ EXAM: OSI 7 layers: Physical(bits) → DataLink(frames) → Network(packets) → Transport(segments) → Session → Presentation → Application
★ EXAM: Devices — Hub/Repeater(L1), Switch/Bridge(L2), Router(L3). Each uses diff addresses
vs: TCP (reliable, ordered, connection handshake, flow control) vs UDP (fast, no guarantee, connectionless)
★ EXAM: TCP 3-way handshake — SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Establishes connection before data
★ EXAM: Subnetting — usable hosts = 2^H - 2 (subtract network + broadcast addresses)
★ EXAM: CIDR /24 = 255.255.255.0 = 256 IPs, 254 usable. /25 = 128 IPs, 126 usable
⚠️ TRAP: x.x.x.0 is network address, x.x.x.255 is broadcast. Neither can be assigned to hosts
vs: RIP (distance vector, max 15 hops) vs OSPF (link state, Dijkstra, fast convergence)
★ EXAM: BGP — path vector protocol for inter-AS (inter-domain) routing on the Internet
★ EXAM: NAT — maps private IPs (192.168.x.x) to public IP. Conserves IPv4 addresses
★ EXAM: DNS resolution — Client→Recursive Resolver→Root→TLD(.com)→Authoritative→IP returned
★ EXAM: DHCP — auto-assigns IP via DORA: Discover→Offer→Request→Acknowledge
vs: IPv4 (32-bit, 4.3B addresses, dotted decimal) vs IPv6 (128-bit, hex notation, enormous space)
★ EXAM: MAC address — 48-bit physical NIC address. Used at Layer 2 for local frame delivery
★ EXAM: Ports — HTTP(80), HTTPS(443), DNS(53), SSH(22), FTP(20/21), SMTP(25)
⚠️ TRAP: Ping uses ICMP (Layer 3). ICMP does NOT use TCP or UDP ports
★ EXAM: ARP — resolves known IP address to MAC address on local LAN segment
★ EXAM: TCP flow control — sliding window mechanism prevents sender from overwhelming receiver
⚠️ TRAP: SSL/TLS operates at Presentation layer (L6), NOT Application layer
★ EXAM: CIA Triad — Confidentiality(only authorized), Integrity(unaltered), Availability(accessible)
vs: Symmetric (same key, fast, AES) vs Asymmetric (public+private pair, slower, RSA)
★ EXAM: AES = symmetric block cipher. RSA = asymmetric. AES for data, RSA for key exchange
⚠️ TRAP: Hashing is ONE-WAY (SHA-256). Encryption is TWO-WAY (reversible with key)
★ EXAM: Digital signature — sender encrypts hash with PRIVATE key. Verifier uses PUBLIC key
⚠️ TRAP: Digital signature uses PRIVATE to sign (opposite of encryption public→encrypt)
★ EXAM: PKI — Certificate Authority issues digital certificates binding identity to public key
★ EXAM: SQL injection — malicious SQL in input. Fix: parameterized queries / prepared statements
★ EXAM: XSS — inject JS into webpage. Stored XSS persists in DB. Reflected XSS in URL params
★ EXAM: CSRF — tricks authenticated browser into unwanted request. Fix: CSRF tokens
Phishing — fake site/email steals credentials. Spear phishing = targeted at specific person
★ EXAM: MITM — attacker intercepts between two parties. Mitigated by SSL/TLS encryption
★ EXAM: Firewall types — Packet filter(headers), Stateful(connections), Application proxy(content)
vs: IDS (detects + alerts) vs IPS (detects + BLOCKS/prevents the traffic)
⚠️ TRAP: Worm self-replicates without user action. Virus requires user to execute infected file
★ EXAM: DDoS — botnet floods target. Exhausts availability. Mitigate with rate limiting/CDN
★ EXAM: MFA — something you know(password) + have(token) + are(biometric). Min 2 factors
★ EXAM: OWASP Top 10 #1: Broken Access Control. Users bypass authorization to reach unauthorized data
vs: Waterfall (sequential, rigid, fixed requirements) vs Agile (iterative sprints, flexible, customer feedback)
★ EXAM: Spiral model — risk-driven. Repeated cycles: planning → risk analysis → engineering → evaluation
V-Model — each dev phase has corresponding test phase. Validation at each level
vs: Functional requirements (what system does — login, search) vs Non-functional (how — speed, security)
★ EXAM: Use Case diagram — oval(use case), stick figure(actor), rectangle(system boundary)
★ EXAM: Sequence diagram — lifelines(vertical), messages(horizontal arrows), time flows downward
vs: Use Case (actor goals/interactions) vs Activity (step-by-step workflow/process flow)
★ EXAM: Class diagram — top:ClassName, mid:-attributes, bottom:+methods. Shows relationships
★ EXAM: SOLID — S:Single Responsibility, O:Open-Closed, L:Liskov Substitution, I:Interface Segregation, D:Dependency Inversion
vs: Low coupling (modules independent — GOOD) vs High cohesion (module focused — GOOD)
★ EXAM: Testing levels — Unit(function) → Integration(modules) → System(whole app) → UAT(user)
⚠️ TRAP: Unit testing = DEVELOPERS. UAT = USERS/clients accepting the deliverable
vs: Black box (test without code knowledge) vs White box (test with full code visibility)
★ EXAM: Refactoring — improve internal structure WITHOUT changing external behavior
⚠️ TRAP: Verification = "building it RIGHT" (process). Validation = "building the RIGHT thing" (product)
Design patterns — Singleton(one instance), Factory(creation), Observer(subscription), MVC(separation)
TDD — Test-Driven Development. Write test FIRST, then code to pass it. Red→Green→Refactor
★ EXAM: PM lifecycle — Initiating → Planning → Executing → Monitoring/Controlling → Closing
★ EXAM: WBS — hierarchical decomposition: Project → Phases → Deliverables → Work Packages → Tasks
★ EXAM: Critical Path — longest path through network. Zero float. Any delay → project delay
★ EXAM: Float/Slack = Late Start − Early Start. Critical activities have float = 0
★ EXAM: CPI = EV/AC. >1 = under budget, <1 = over budget. SPI = EV/PV. >1 = ahead
★ EXAM: EAC = BAC/CPI — estimated total cost at completion based on current performance
⚠️ TRAP: Critical path tasks have ZERO float. Adding resources to non-critical tasks doesn't help
★ EXAM: Risk responses — Avoid(eliminate), Transfer(insurance), Mitigate(reduce), Accept(acknowledge)
★ EXAM: Risk matrix — Probability × Impact = Risk Score. High score = priority treatment
★ EXAM: Stakeholder matrix — Power vs Interest grid. High/High = Manage Closely
vs: Agile (iterative sprints, flexible scope) vs Waterfall (sequential, fixed requirements)
★ EXAM: Scrum roles — Product Owner(priorities), Scrum Master(facilitator), Dev Team(builds)
⚠️ TRAP: Scrum Master is NOT a project manager — facilitator only, no authority over team
★ EXAM: Project Charter — formally authorizes project and grants PM authority
★ EXAM: Triple Constraint — Scope, Time, Cost. Change one → affects the other two
⚠️ TRAP: Gold plating = adding unrequested features. Wastes resources, increases risk. Bad practice
COCOMO — estimates effort in person-months from code size (KLOC). Basic/Intermediate/Detailed
★ EXAM: State space — initial state + operators + goal test + path cost = search problem
★ EXAM: BFS — level-by-level, queue. Complete, optimal for equal costs. O(b^d) time+space
★ EXAM: DFS — depth-first, stack/recursion. NOT complete (infinite loops). O(bm) space
vs: BFS (finds shortest path, high memory) vs DFS (deep-first, low memory, may not terminate)
IDDFS — iterative deepening. BFS completeness + DFS memory efficiency. Best uninformed search
UCS — Uniform Cost Search. Expands lowest path cost first. Optimal but slow
★ EXAM: A* search — f(n)=g(n)+h(n). Optimal if h(n) is admissible (never overestimates)
⚠️ TRAP: Greedy Best-First uses only h(n). NOT optimal, NOT complete. A* IS optimal
★ EXAM: Admissible heuristic — h(n) ≤ h*(n) where h* is true cost. Required for A* optimality
★ EXAM: Bayes theorem — P(A|B) = P(B|A)×P(A)/P(B). Updates belief with evidence
★ EXAM: Bayesian network — DAG of conditional dependencies. Nodes=variables, edges=influence
★ EXAM: Propositional logic — AND(∧), OR(∨), NOT(¬), IMPLIES(→), IFF(↔)
★ EXAM: Modus Ponens — if P→Q and P is true, then Q is true. Most basic inference rule
⚠️ TRAP: P→Q is false ONLY when P=true and Q=false. All other combinations = true
Conditional independence — X⊥Y|Z means X and Y independent given Z. Key for Bayesian nets
★ EXAM: 3 ML types — Supervised(labeled→predict), Unsupervised(unlabeled→patterns), Reinforcement(agent+reward)
vs: Classification (discrete output — cat/dog) vs Regression (continuous — price prediction)
★ EXAM: Linear regression — y=mx+b. Minimizes sum of squared errors. Continuous prediction
Logistic regression — classification via sigmoid function. Output = probability [0,1]
Decision tree — splits by feature values. Leaf=class label. Prone to overfitting
Random Forest — ensemble of decision trees. Majority vote. Reduces overfitting
KNN — classify by K nearest neighbors majority vote. Lazy learner. No training phase
★ EXAM: Bias-variance tradeoff — high bias=underfitting, high variance=overfitting
⚠️ TRAP: Overfitting = high training accuracy but low test accuracy. Model memorized data
vs: Train set(fit model) vs Validation set(tune hyperparams) vs Test set(final evaluation)
★ EXAM: K-Fold CV — split into K parts, train on K-1, test on 1, rotate K times, average
★ EXAM: Accuracy=(TP+TN)/all. Precision=TP/(TP+FP). Recall=TP/(TP+FN). F1=harmonic mean
⚠️ TRAP: High accuracy misleading with imbalanced data (99% class A → 99% accuracy is trivial)
★ EXAM: ROC curve — TPR vs FPR plot. AUC = area under curve. AUC=1.0 is perfect classifier
★ EXAM: K-Means — assign K centroids, assign points to nearest, recalculate centroids, repeat
PCA — dimensionality reduction. Finds directions of maximum variance. Unsupervised
★ EXAM: Neural net — input→hidden→output layers. Each node: z=wx+b, a=activation(z)
Activation functions — Sigmoid(0-1), ReLU(max(0,x) most common), Tanh(-1 to 1)
★ EXAM: Gradient descent — w = w - α·∂J/∂w. α=learning rate. Minimizes loss iteratively
⚠️ TRAP: Deep learning needs large data + compute. Not always better. Interpretability problem
★ EXAM: Revisit every RED-rated (conf=1) topic from Days 0-5. Close all knowledge gaps
★ EXAM: Revisit every YELLOW-rated topic. Convert to green via active recall
★ EXAM: Write one-line definition for each of the 13 courses from memory
Practice scenario questions — given situation, identify which concept applies
Quick-fire self-quiz — cover topic text, recite definition from memory
Focus on formula topics — EVM, Big-O, subnetting, Bayes, gradient descent
Review all "⚠️ TRAP" topics — these are the most common wrong answers
Review all "vs:" comparison topics — exams love "what's the difference" questions
★ EXAM: Re-read own notes only — zero new material after 8pm
★ EXAM: Prepare exam ID, pens, calculator, registration documents tonight
★ EXAM: Sleep 8 hours — memory consolidation happens during sleep. Non-negotiable
Light meal tonight + good breakfast tomorrow. Brain needs glucose for performance
★ EXAM: Arrive 20 min early. Read ALL questions before answering. Spot easy ones first
★ EXAM: For MCQ: eliminate obviously wrong answers first. If stuck, mark and move on
Time management — allocate minutes per question. Don't spend 10min on one question
